Chapter 4: Conditional Probability and Bayes' Theorem

4.1 Conditional Probability

In many applications, we want to compute the probability of an event given that another event has already occurred. This leads to the concept of conditional probability.

Definition 4.1.1. If A and B are events with P(B) > 0, the conditional probability of A given B is defined as:

  P(A | B) = P(A ∩ B) / P(B).

Intuitively, P(A | B) represents the probability of A occurring, given that B has occurred. We restrict our sample space to B and look at the proportion of B that also belongs to A.

Example 4.1.2. A fair six-sided die is rolled. Let A = {the roll is even} = {2, 4, 6} and B = {the roll is at most 4} = {1, 2, 3, 4}. What is P(A | B)?

Solution. P(A ∩ B) = P({2, 4}) = 2/6 = 1/3. P(B) = 4/6 = 2/3. Therefore P(A | B) = (1/3)/(2/3) = 1/2.

This makes sense: given that the roll is at most 4, the possible outcomes are {1, 2, 3, 4}, and exactly two of these (2 and 4) are even.

Example 4.1.3. Two cards are drawn from a standard 52-card deck without replacement. What is the probability that the second card is a heart given that the first card was a heart?

Solution. Let A = {second card is a heart} and B = {first card is a heart}. After drawing one heart, 12 hearts remain among 51 cards. So P(A | B) = 12/51 = 4/17.

4.2 Independence

Definition 4.2.1. Events A and B are independent if P(A ∩ B) = P(A) * P(B). Equivalently (when P(B) > 0), A and B are independent if P(A | B) = P(A) — knowing B occurred does not change the probability of A.

Warning: Independence and mutual exclusivity are different concepts! If A and B are mutually exclusive (A ∩ B = empty set) and both have positive probability, then P(A ∩ B) = 0 != P(A)P(B), so they are not independent.

Example 4.2.2. A fair coin is tossed twice. Let A = {first toss is heads} and B = {second toss is heads}. Then P(A) = 1/2, P(B) = 1/2, P(A ∩ B) = 1/4 = P(A)P(B). So A and B are independent. The outcome of the first toss does not affect the second.

4.3 The Law of Total Probability

Theorem 4.3.1 (Law of Total Probability). Let B_1, B_2, ..., B_n be a partition of the sample space (mutually exclusive, exhaustive events with P(B_i) > 0). Then for any event A:

  P(A) = sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A | B_i) P(B_i).

Proof. Since the B_i partition the sample space, A = A ∩ Omega = A ∩ (B_1 ∪ ... ∪ B_n) = (A ∩ B_1) ∪ ... ∪ (A ∩ B_n), and these are disjoint. So P(A) = sum P(A ∩ B_i) = sum P(A | B_i) P(B_i). QED.

Example 4.3.2. A factory has two machines: Machine I produces 60% of items, Machine II produces 40%. Machine I has a 3% defect rate, Machine II has a 5% defect rate. What is the overall defect rate?

Solution. Let D = {item is defective}, B_1 = {from Machine I}, B_2 = {from Machine II}. Then P(D) = P(D | B_1)P(B_1) + P(D | B_2)P(B_2) = (0.03)(0.60) + (0.05)(0.40) = 0.018 + 0.020 = 0.038. The overall defect rate is 3.8%.

4.4 Bayes' Theorem

Theorem 4.4.1 (Bayes' Theorem). Let B_1, ..., B_n be a partition of the sample space with P(B_i) > 0 for all i. Then for any event A with P(A) > 0:

  P(B_j | A) = P(A | B_j) P(B_j) / sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A | B_i) P(B_i).

Proof. By definition, P(B_j | A) = P(A ∩ B_j)/P(A) = P(A | B_j)P(B_j)/P(A). Substituting the Law of Total Probability for P(A) gives the result. QED.

Bayes' theorem is remarkable because it lets us "reverse" conditional probabilities — we go from P(A | B_j) (the likelihood of the evidence given a hypothesis) to P(B_j | A) (the probability of the hypothesis given the evidence).

Example 4.4.2. Continuing the factory example: if a randomly selected item is found to be defective, what is the probability it came from Machine I?

Solution. P(B_1 | D) = P(D | B_1)P(B_1) / P(D) = (0.03)(0.60) / 0.038 = 0.018 / 0.038 ≈ 0.474. So there is about a 47.4% chance the defective item came from Machine I.

Note that even though Machine I produces more items, Machine II's higher defect rate means Machine II is almost equally likely to be the source of a defect.

Example 4.4.3 (Medical testing). A disease affects 1% of the population. A diagnostic test has 99% sensitivity (true positive rate) and 95% specificity (true negative rate, meaning 5% false positive rate). If a person tests positive, what is the probability they actually have the disease?

Solution. Let D = {has disease}, T+ = {tests positive}. P(D) = 0.01, P(D^c) = 0.99. P(T+ | D) = 0.99, P(T+ | D^c) = 0.05.

By the Law of Total Probability: P(T+) = P(T+ | D)P(D) + P(T+ | D^c)P(D^c) = (0.99)(0.01) + (0.05)(0.99) = 0.0099 + 0.0495 = 0.0594.

By Bayes' Theorem: P(D | T+) = (0.99)(0.01) / 0.0594 = 0.0099 / 0.0594 ≈ 0.167.

Only about 16.7% of people who test positive actually have the disease! This surprising result illustrates the base rate fallacy: when a disease is rare, even a good test produces many false positives relative to true positives.

4.5 Combinatorial Probability

Many probability problems reduce to counting favorable outcomes. The key counting tools are:

Permutations: The number of ways to arrange r objects from n is P(n, r) = n! / (n - r)!.

Combinations: The number of ways to choose r objects from n (order doesn't matter) is C(n, r) = n! / (r!(n - r)!), also written as "n choose r".

Example 4.5.1. A committee of 3 is chosen from 10 people. What is the probability that a specific person (say Alice) is on the committee?

Solution. Total committees: C(10, 3) = 120. Committees containing Alice: C(9, 2) = 36 (choose 2 more from the remaining 9). Probability: 36/120 = 3/10.

Alternatively, the probability that Alice is chosen for any of the 3 spots is 3/10 by symmetry.
